Did you know we keep a Beta-tester of the Month wall of fame at Memrise? To be eligible, send us detailed reports of any problems you find in the latest build. We might just throw in a coffee mug.
This is the Home screen. A dashboard where you can continue learning from where you left off. You can also find useful tools like review modes.
Content is organised into units, called Scenarios. Here you can explore them and track the ones you are learning, or have completed.

"Your Memrise membership applies to your username rather than specific languages. This means that you'll be able to access all content across all languages available on the app and website."
On Memrise, you can access and learn as many languages as you want at the same time!
"If you select the Lifetime plan, the amount will be charged as a one-off upfront payment without any renewals and you'll have Pro status forever."
Yes! You can access your Memrise profile and use your Pro membership on more than one device: simply log in with the same details on your iOS and Android mobile apps, or on your computer.

Yes, you can cancel your auto-renewal any time after you have been charged for your subscription. The paid features will remain active until the end of your membership period. If you purchase a Lifetime plan, you won’t need to cancel your auto-renewal as it’s a one-off purchase.

You can still access all of the Community Courses on the Memrise website only. All the content from the official courses are in the new experience, but each level is now called a \"scenario\".

"There is a recommended path that you can progress through. The content on this path is the same as the courses you learned on the old Memrise. So you can track your progress through “Scenarios completed” there.
You will also now see a weekly progress tracker on your dashboard that shows your words learned, videos watched and conversations completed. We will be adding more progress tools soon!"
"How do I know how I'm making progress when I don't have a sequence of courses from 1-7?"

"If you are learning an official Memrise course, they are now only available through the new UI.
If you are enrolled in a Community Course, these courses are now web-exclusive. Opting for the transition to the new Memrise experience means forfeiting access to Community Courses."
How do you go back to the old Memrise?

"All your progress on Memrise courses is still here - see how it's displayed differently under the 'Your Progress' section of this page. Progress on community courses is still available on the Memrise Classic website. You can see all your completed Word Lists on the “scenarios” tab, and you can also see a “recommended path” there too. The content on the recommended path is the same as the path through the Memrise courses 1-7 on the old Memrise. In the rare technical case that your progress hasn't transferred over, please let us know: %s so we can resolve this."
"I've lost my progress, what has happened?"

"Knowing grammar can help you in your language learning journey. But it is not essential: children don’t learn grammar and get fluent; Latin scholars know all the grammar but can’t speak a fluent sentence out loud. Knowing the grammar isn’t needed, and it doesn’t guarantee success in the spoken language.
Memrise is all about giving you the things that are essential: enough practice understanding native speakers using the words you’ve learned, and enough practice using those words to communicate what you want to say.
This is the unique value that Memrise delivers, so we’re focusing in on doing that better and better, and are putting less focus on things that aren’t essential for learning a language, like grammar mode."

"Because the new Memrise experience is so strongly about getting the practice you need in order to master a given scenario, you need to be able to watch loads of videos and chat with the AI chatbot. Unfortunately, that isn’t possible offline.
Additionally, we are seeing less and less use of offline mode as internet connections on e.g. airplanes and subways get more ubiquitous, reducing the need for offline mode.
We know this is painful in some situations, but we believe that this is the right moment to make the change."

"Most people are learning languages to be able to master very specific situations (scenarios) in which they need to have a conversation. Courses on the old Memrise were broken into levels, which generally related to a specific scenario “basic greetings”, “ordering in a restaurant” etc.
The new Memrise experience is all about getting the practice you need to move from knowing vocab to understanding native speakers and being able to speak to them with confidence, using the words you’ve learned. In other words, everything you need in order to master that scenario.
That’s why we’ve shifted from calling these “levels” within linear courses, and instead making it clear that you can choose any scenario that you want to master. This has actually always been possible in Memrise - we’re just now emphasizing that is the best way to learn on Memrise."
Why are Word Lists called scenarios?

%s days of Memrise in a row
TIL: Writing notes on paper makes you learn better than typing notes.
I am currently not wearing any trousers.
My diet consists mostly of Gavorkian Beetles.
Roses are red. But only according to your primitive cone cells.
Every semicolon I have ever used has been a shot in the dark.
"You're supposed to become a pro after 10,000 hours but I'm 175,200 hours into life and still have no idea what I'm doing."
Humans are really bad at recharging, it takes about 8 hours charge for 16 hours of use.
"There should be confetti in tires so when there is a blow out it's still kind of an okay day."
Sneezing is like using sonar to find polite people.
%s day of Memrise in a row
TIL: The sanskrit word for ‘war’ literally translates as ‘a desire for more cows’.
A group of squid should be called a squad.
"TIL: The Finnish word 'kalsarikännit' means to get drunk at home, alone, in your underwear."
TIL: The average pencil can draw a line 35 miles long before you run out of lead.
TIL: In the English language, there is a new word created every 98 minutes or about 14.7 words per day.
At any one time, up to 75,000 people are learning the same word.
3 people just opened Memrise at the exact same time as you. You should form a club.
TIL: All American astronauts have to learn to speak Russian before they go to space, so they can read the ISS manuals.
TIL: People think more rationally in their second language and make better decisions.
